hero_headline: Back to school with AI: clearer guidance, smarter habits, and honest watch-outs
intro: Welcome back. As we start a new year, the national conversation has shifted from whether to use AI to how to use it well. This month the U.S. Department of Education put out real guidance, and the loudest voices in the field are landing on the same point we keep making, AI is a thinking partner, not an answer machine. There is also a fair amount to be careful about, so we are giving the watch-outs their own spot.

items.title: U.S. Department of Education Issues Guidance on Artificial Intelligence Use in Schools
items.takeaway: The feds clarified that districts can use federal grant dollars for responsible AI like tutoring and personalized materials, with privacy and stakeholder input front and center.

items.title: From Policy to Practice: How AI is Shaping the Future of Education
items.takeaway: A useful bridge from the big policy ideas to what they actually look like in a building, helpful as we think about our own AI direction.

items.title: OpenAI's education head says students should use ChatGPT as a tool, not an answer machine
items.takeaway: The message we have been making all along, straight from the source: the goal is productive struggle, not handing the work to the bot.

items.title: Khan Academy CEO predicts AI in the classroom will be like 5 amazing graduate students assisting teachers
items.takeaway: A hopeful framing for the year: AI as a team of tireless assistants that frees teachers to do the human work, not a replacement for them.

items.title: As schools embrace AI, an educator offers top tips, cautions, and predictions
items.takeaway: A practical, classroom-tested list of where to start and where to be careful, a good five-minute read before you plan your first AI lesson.

sections.name: Proceed With Caution
sections.note: A standing spot for the honest watch-outs. AI is a tool, not a shortcut around judgment.

items.title: So long, study guides? The AI industry is going after students
items.takeaway: AI companies are aggressively marketing study shortcuts straight to students, so it is worth naming for kids the difference between learning and outsourcing.

items.title: AI Teacher Assistants Are Useful but Can Pose Risks in Classroom, Report Finds
items.takeaway: These tools save time, but a new report flags bias and accuracy gaps, so keep a human eye on anything they generate.

items.title: Researchers Posed as a Teen in Crisis. AI Gave Them Harmful Advice Half the Time
items.takeaway: A sobering reminder that general chatbots are not safe for students in crisis, worth knowing before you point any kid toward one for support.

items.title: Not Cheating, Learning: Students on Using AI Ethically
items.takeaway: Students themselves talk through where the line sits between using AI to learn and using it to cheat, a good listen for any teacher building expectations this year.

editor_pick.title: OpenAI's education head says students should use ChatGPT as a tool, not an answer machine
editor_pick.blurb: If you read one thing this month, make it this. The person leading education at OpenAI is telling students the same thing we are: the value is in the struggle, not the shortcut. That is the whole game this year.
